nodes.name: scientific name acceptance
nodes.desc: The currently accepted binomial (or trinomial) under the relevant code (ICN), with explicit accepted-vs-synonym status. This is the canonical handle every other link in the entire chain keys off. GEOCON stores it as species.accepted_name bu

nodes.name: authorship citation
nodes.desc: The full author citation including parenthetical (basionym) author and combining author, e.g. Crocus cancellatus subsp. mazziaricus (Herb.) B.Mathew. GEOCON has a flat species.authority / accepted_name_authority string but does not validate

nodes.name: basionym and protologue
nodes.desc: The basionym (original name on which the current combination rests) plus the protologue (the original publication: journal, volume, page, year of the validating description). For geophytes this is frequently an 18th-19th C. European flora,

nodes.name: nomenclatural type
nodes.desc: The type method that fixes the name's application: holotype (+ isotypes, lectotype, neotype, epitype, syntypes) — which physical specimen IS the name. Distinct from the broader voucher set: this is the ONE element that legally anchors the n

nodes.name: synonymy set
nodes.desc: The full set of names that have been applied to this taxon (homotypic + heterotypic synonyms), each with its own type and status. Geophyte genera are synonymy-heavy: a single Tulipa or Crocus species can carry 20+ synonyms from regional ove

nodes.name: homotypic heterotypic partition
nodes.desc: Partition of the synonymy into homotypic (same type, pure nomenclatural shuffles) vs heterotypic (different type, a taxonomic judgement that two entities are conspecific). The heterotypic edges are exactly where circumscription is contested

nodes.name: orthography and validity
nodes.desc: Whether the name is validly published, legitimate, and correctly spelled under ICN (vs nomen nudum, nomen illegitimum, isonym, orthographic variant). Also gender agreement of the epithet with the genus.

nodes.name: conserved rejected names
nodes.desc: Whether the name is formally conserved (nom. cons.) or rejected (nom. rej.) under the ICN Appendices, including conserved types. Affects which of two competing names wins.

nodes.name: hybrid nothotaxon status
nodes.desc: Whether the entity is a hybrid (nothotaxon, notho-genus/species), including the parental formula (e.g. Crocus x paulineae). Many cultivated/horticulturally important geophytes (Narcissus, Tulipa, Galanthus, Iris) involve hybrids, and natura

nodes.name: infraspecific taxa
nodes.desc: The accepted subspecies / varieties / forms under the species, each a distinct chain coordinate with its own type, distribution, and possibly its own conservation status. Geophyte taxa are frequently recognized at subspecies rank (e.g. the

nodes.name: vernacular names
nodes.desc: Common/local names in relevant languages (Turkish, Greek, Persian, Arabic, English), including the names used in trade and in traditional knowledge. For geophytes these map to bulb-trade and ethnobotanical usage (e.g. 'sahlep' for Orchis/Op

nodes.name: type specimen voucher
nodes.desc: The physical type material as a curated, citable object (institution, barcode, image, georeference) — the specimen-layer counterpart of the nomenclatural_type act. Distinguishes the OBJECT (a sheet at K) from the ACT (designating it as lect

nodes.name: herbarium voucher set
nodes.desc: The full set of identified herbarium specimens documenting the species across its range and time — the material basis for any morphometric or distributional claim. This is GEOCON's LIVE herbarium_specimens table.

nodes.name: living collection voucher identity
nodes.desc: The verified identity of ex-situ LIVING material (a bulb/corm in a botanic-garden bed or seed-bank lot), proving that the accession in the propagation/cultivation links IS this species. Without it, the Connection edge (welding propagation->

nodes.name: ex situ provenance identity
nodes.desc: The wild-collection provenance and chain-of-custody of ex-situ material: was this accession wild-sourced (with collection permit / ABS) or of garden/horticultural origin? Affects both conservation value and Nagoya/ABS compliance of any down

nodes.name: iconography and illustrations
nodes.desc: Authoritative botanical illustrations and historic iconography that serve as identity references, sometimes as the type itself (older names can be typified on an illustration). For ornamental geophytes (Tulipa, Iris, Lilium) there is a rich

nodes.name: georeferenced occurrence identity
nodes.desc: Identified, georeferenced occurrence records (human observations + specimens) that confirm the named entity occurs where claimed — the identity-validation slice of occurrence data (as opposed to the distribution branch's range modelling).

nodes.name: dna barcode
nodes.desc: Standard plant DNA barcode loci (rbcL, matK, trnH-psbA, and ITS/ITS2) sequenced from vouchered material, enabling sequence-based identification. Critical for geophytes because dormant or fragmentary material (a bulb in trade, a corm with no

nodes.name: phylogenetic placement
nodes.desc: The species' position in a published phylogeny — its clade, sister-taxon relationships, and the tree topology supporting them. For geophytes this maps onto the molecular reclassifications that reshaped Asparagales, Liliales, and the segrega

nodes.name: monophyly status
nodes.desc: Whether the species (and its genus) is recovered as monophyletic, paraphyletic, or polyphyletic in molecular analyses — i.e. whether the current circumscription survives phylogenetic testing. Geophyte genera have been heavily re-circumscrib

nodes.name: sequence accessions
nodes.desc: The inventory of public sequence data available for the species (which loci/genes/genomes are in GenBank/ENA), independent of any single phylogeny — a measure of molecular characterization depth.

nodes.name: phylogenomic markers
nodes.desc: Beyond Sanger barcodes: target-capture (Angiosperms353), plastome, RAD-seq, or transcriptome marker sets used for fine-scale and population-level delimitation. Increasingly the standard for resolving recent geophyte radiations.

nodes.name: molecular dating node
nodes.desc: The estimated divergence time / crown age of the lineage from dated molecular phylogenies — places the species in evolutionary time and informs whether it is a neo-endemic (recently diverged, e.g. a Mediterranean glacial-refugium radiation)

nodes.name: species delimitation evidence
nodes.desc: The explicit, methodologically-stated case for where one species ends and the next begins: integrative taxonomy combining molecular (e.g. BPP, ASAP, GMYC), morphological, and cytological lines. The crux of the cryptic-species problem in Cro

nodes.name: reference genome or plastome
nodes.desc: A whole-genome or complete plastid-genome assembly for the species — the deepest molecular characterization, enabling everything from biosynthetic-gene-cluster mining to definitive identification. Rare for geophytes but rising (e.g. publish

nodes.name: storage organ architecture
nodes.desc: THE geophyte-defining link: the precise type and architecture of the perennating underground storage organ — true bulb (tunicate vs scaly/imbricate), corm, tuber (stem vs root tuber), rhizome, bulbil/bulbous-base — plus its replacement stra

nodes.name: tunic and coat morphology
nodes.desc: The covering of the storage organ — tunic texture (membranous, fibrous, reticulate-fibrous, coriaceous) and corm-tunic structure. A primary DIAGNOSTIC character in Crocus (tunic type is the first split in the genus) and Gladiolus/Romulea.

nodes.name: root system type
nodes.desc: Root architecture associated with the geophyte habit: contractile roots (pull the organ to depth), fleshy storage roots (root tubers, e.g. Eranthis, some Ranunculus geophytes, Ophrys/Orchis sahlep tubers), fibrous vs fleshy, and seasonal ro

nodes.name: leaf and shoot morphology
nodes.desc: Leaf characters diagnostic in geophytes: number, shape, the characteristic white/pale median stripe (Crocus), basal vs cauline arrangement, channeling, hysteranthy vs synanthy (leaves before/with/after flowers — a major phenological-diagnos

nodes.name: floral morphology and formula
nodes.desc: Floral architecture and the floral formula: perianth (tepals — geophytes are overwhelmingly monocot petaloids), perianth-tube length, stamen number/insertion, style branching (the saffron 3-branched style of Crocus sativus is both diagnosti

nodes.name: fruit and seed morphology
nodes.desc: Capsule type and dehiscence, seed shape/size/ornamentation, presence of elaiosomes (myrmecochory is common in geophytes — Cyclamen, Corydalis, many Liliaceae), and seed-coat micromorphology used in identification and in seed-banking.

nodes.name: micromorphology and anatomy
nodes.desc: Internal/surface micro-characters: storage-organ tissue anatomy (starch-grain morphology, mucilage canals, raphide/calcium-oxalate crystal idioblasts — diagnostic AND toxicologically relevant), epidermal/stomatal characters, leaf cross-sect

nodes.name: palynology pollen
nodes.desc: Pollen morphology (aperture type, exine sculpturing, size) — diagnostic at genus/species level in several geophyte groups and used in melissopalynology (e.g. tracing geophyte-derived honey/pollen) and in fossil/subfossil identification.

nodes.name: chromosome count
nodes.desc: The somatic chromosome number (2n) — a foundational cytological identity character. Geophyte genera are cytologically hyper-variable: Crocus alone spans a huge basic-number range, and counts are routinely used to distinguish otherwise-crypt

nodes.name: ploidy level
nodes.desc: The ploidy (diploid, triploid, tetraploid, polyploid series) inferred from chromosome counts or measured by flow cytometry. Decisive in geophytes: many crops/ornamentals are sterile triploids (Crocus sativus 3n, many cultivated Narcissus/Tu

nodes.name: karyotype and genome size
nodes.desc: The full karyotype (chromosome morphology, arm ratios, satellite/SAT chromosomes, B-chromosomes) and the C-value / genome size (1C in pg or Mbp). Geophytes include some of the largest known angiosperm genomes (Fritillaria, some Liliaceae),

nodes.name: pollination breeding system identity
nodes.desc: The reproductive/breeding system as an identity-relevant trait: selfing vs outcrossing, self-incompatibility, apomixis, dichogamy, and pollinator syndrome. Shapes whether morphological boundaries are maintained (outcrossers blur; selfers/ap

nodes.name: diagnostic character matrix
nodes.desc: The integrated, structured set of diagnostic characters (a character × state matrix) that actually separates this species from its closest relatives — the synthesis that a taxonomic key or DELTA/Xper dataset encodes. The morphological backb

nodes.name: circumscription stability
nodes.desc: A SYNTHESIS/GATE link (not a single datum): how stable the species' boundary currently is, integrating synonymy churn, monophyly status, active revisions, and integrative-delimitation congruence. Proposed to act as a MULTIPLIER on the speci

nodes.name: native range countries
nodes.desc: Set of countries/political units where the geophyte occurs natively (not introduced). The coarsest 'where' link.

nodes.name: tdwg regions
nodes.desc: Subnational botanical distribution as TDWG WGSRPD level-3/4 region codes (e.g. Turkey-in-Asia split into 5 WCVP regions) — finer than country, coarser than coordinates.

nodes.name: point occurrences
nodes.desc: Georeferenced point occurrence records (lat/long) of the species in the wild — the true distribution at coordinate resolution.

nodes.name: range metrics eoo aoo
nodes.desc: Extent of Occurrence (EOO, convex hull km2) and Area of Occupancy (AOO, occupied 2km grid km2) — the quantitative range-size metrics IUCN B-criterion uses.

nodes.name: endemism class
nodes.desc: Endemism status & grain: point endemic / narrow endemic / regional endemic / widespread; flags single-site CR geophytes.

nodes.name: elevation band
nodes.desc: Altitudinal range (min/max/optimum m a.s.l.) the geophyte occupies — strongly diagnostic for montane bulbs and alpine geophytes.

nodes.name: introduced naturalised range
nodes.desc: Where the geophyte occurs as introduced/naturalised/invasive (e.g. Oxalis pes-caprae, Watsonia, some Gladiolus) — distinct from native range so it never inflates conservation priority.

nodes.name: historical range contraction
nodes.desc: Documented loss of historical localities — extirpated populations, range edge retreat over decades.

nodes.name: vegetation type
nodes.desc: Habitat / plant-community type the geophyte grows in (e.g. phrygana/garrigue, steppe, oak woodland understory, alpine turf, vernal pool, serpentine outcrop, screes).

nodes.name: geology parent material
nodes.desc: Underlying geology / parent rock the geophyte associates with — limestone, serpentine/ultramafic, gypsum, volcanic, dolomite. Many narrow-endemic geophytes are edaphic specialists.

nodes.name: soil profile
nodes.desc: Soil physical & chemical profile of native sites: pH, texture/drainage, organic matter, N/P/K, salinity, Ca, heavy metals (for ultramafic specialists), depth to storage-organ zone.

nodes.name: hydrology water regime
nodes.desc: Seasonal water availability the geophyte is adapted to: summer-drought (Mediterranean bulbs), seasonal flooding (vernal-pool geophytes), snowmelt-fed, monsoon. The driver of the dormancy clock.

nodes.name: fire regime
nodes.desc: Native fire frequency/intensity and the geophyte's fire response — many geophytes are pyrophytic post-fire flowerers (fire-stimulated flowering in Cape & Mediterranean bulbs, e.g. Cyrtanthus, Boophone).

nodes.name: microtopography aspect
nodes.desc: Fine-scale position: slope, aspect (N/S-facing), rock-crevice vs open turf, microsite that buffers the storage organ — defines microrefugia for narrow endemics.

nodes.name: microclimate
nodes.desc: Near-ground microclimate the storage organ and emergent shoot actually experience: soil temperature at bulb depth, surface vs canopy temperature, humidity, frost exposure, snow-cover duration.

nodes.name: light environment
nodes.desc: Light regime: full-sun open habitat vs deciduous-woodland understory geophyte exploiting the spring light window before canopy closes (vernal geophyte strategy).

nodes.name: realised niche
nodes.desc: The species' realised climatic niche: bioclimatic envelope (WorldClim/CHELSA Bio1–Bio19 summary at occurrences) — temperature/precipitation means, seasonality, extremes the geophyte tolerates.

nodes.name: sdm output
nodes.desc: Species distribution / ecological niche model (MaxEnt or similar): predicted suitable-habitat surface, current potential range, variable importance.

nodes.name: range projection
nodes.desc: Projected future range under IPCC SSP scenarios (SSP1-2.6 / SSP2-4.5 / SSP5-8.5): range_size_km2 per scenario and % change vs baseline.

nodes.name: index
nodes.desc: Composite climate-change vulnerability: exposure (projected climate shift) + sensitivity (narrow niche, alpine/edaphic confinement) + low adaptive capacity (poor dispersal, long generation time, clonal reliance).

nodes.name: microrefugia
nodes.desc: Identified microrefugia — pockets (N-facing crevices, snowbeds, gorges) where the geophyte may persist as macroclimate warms; assisted-colonisation source/target sites.

nodes.name: leafing
nodes.desc: Timing of leaf emergence and the photosynthetic above-ground window (month_idx + intensity). For vernal geophytes this window can be very short.

nodes.name: flowering
nodes.desc: Flowering phenophase timing, duration, and intensity (the signature geophyte event — anthesis month window, e.g. autumn-flowering Colchicum/Sternbergia vs spring Crocus).

nodes.name: fruiting seeding
nodes.desc: Fruiting and seed-maturation/dispersal timing — the seed-collection window for seed-banking and propagation sourcing.

nodes.name: dormancy cycle
nodes.desc: Below-ground dormancy window — when the geophyte is leafless and the storage organ is the only living tissue; the defining geophyte annual rhythm (summer-dormant Mediterranean vs winter-dormant types).

nodes.name: storage organ annual cycle
nodes.desc: The internal storage-organ developmental cycle: when the bulb/corm/tuber depletes, when daughter organs/cormels form, when next-year's bud + flower primordia differentiate inside the organ.

nodes.name: cueing
nodes.desc: Environmental cues that trigger each phenophase: chilling-hours requirement, photoperiod, soil-moisture/rain trigger, temperature threshold for bud-break and anthesis.

nodes.name: breeding system
nodes.desc: Mating system: self-compatible vs obligate outcrosser, dioecy, self-incompatibility type, apomixis. Determines whether isolated/tiny populations can set seed.

nodes.name: floral biology
nodes.desc: Floral morphology & rewards relevant to pollination: flower colour/shape, nectar/pollen reward, scent, anthesis time-of-day, herkogamy/dichogamy.

nodes.name: pollinator identity
nodes.desc: Observed flower visitors / effective pollinators (specific bees, beeflies, moths, sunbirds for some African geophytes) and degree of pollinator specialisation.

nodes.name: dependence
nodes.desc: Degree of pollinator dependence for reproduction: pollinator-limited vs autonomous selfing fallback; quantified pollen-limitation index.

nodes.name: syndrome
nodes.desc: Inferred pollination syndrome (melittophily, psychophily, ornithophily, etc.) — a literature/inferred summary that can stand before direct observation.

nodes.name: vegetative spread
nodes.desc: Clonal/vegetative reproduction via daughter bulbs, cormels, tuber division, rhizome runners, bulbils, stolons — the geophyte's asexual propagation channel.

nodes.name: sexual vs clonal balance
nodes.desc: Relative contribution of seed vs clonal recruitment to population maintenance — determines genetic renewal and response to environmental change.

nodes.name: seed morphology
nodes.desc: Seed morphology & mass: seed size, mass (mg), shape, appendages (elaiosome, wing, aril), embryo type — the physical substrate for dormancy/dispersal/banking.

nodes.name: dormancy class
nodes.desc: Seed dormancy type: physiological (PD — common in temperate geophytes), morphophysiological (MPD — underdeveloped embryo, very common in Liliaceae/Amaryllidaceae), physical, or non-dormant. The single biggest determinant of how to germinate

nodes.name: germination triggers
nodes.desc: Specific cues that break dormancy and trigger germination: cold-moist stratification duration/temperature, warm+cold cycling, smoke/karrikin, GA3, light, after-ripening, scarification.

nodes.name: dispersal mode
nodes.desc: Seed dispersal mode & vector: barochory (gravity — most bulbs drop seed near parent → narrow range), myrmecochory (elaiosome-bearing, ant-dispersed), anemochory, endozoochory, hydrochory, ballistic.

nodes.name: dispersal distance
nodes.desc: Quantified dispersal kernel / typical and maximum dispersal distance — the migration capacity for climate tracking and metapopulation connectivity.

nodes.name: seedbank persistence
nodes.desc: Soil seed-bank persistence: transient vs persistent, longevity in the soil, and ex-situ seed-bank storage behaviour (orthodox/desiccation-tolerant vs recalcitrant).

nodes.name: recruitment
nodes.desc: Natural recruitment & seedling establishment: germination-to-establishment rate in the wild, safe-site requirements, seedling survival bottleneck (often the demographic chokepoint for geophytes).

nodes.name: population size
nodes.desc: Census population size / number of mature individuals per locality — the headline IUCN C/D-criterion number.

nodes.name: subpopulation count
nodes.desc: Number and spatial arrangement of subpopulations / locations (IUCN sense) and degree of fragmentation/isolation.

nodes.name: structure
nodes.desc: Population stage/size structure: ratio of seedlings : juveniles (non-flowering rosettes) : flowering adults : dormant individuals — diagnostic of a healthy vs senescing population.

nodes.name: vital rates
nodes.desc: Demographic vital rates: stage-specific survival, growth/transition probabilities (including dormancy-to-emergence), fecundity — the matrix-model parameters and resulting population growth rate (lambda).

nodes.name: generation length
nodes.desc: Generation length / time-to-first-flowering and longevity — long for geophytes (years-to-decades), which the trust model explicitly flags as making replication unreachable.

nodes.name: population trend
nodes.desc: Population trend over time: increasing / stable / declining / fluctuating, with % change — the IUCN A-criterion input.

nodes.name: diversity
nodes.desc: Within-population genetic diversity: heterozygosity, allelic richness, inbreeding coefficient — low in tiny/clonal geophyte populations.

nodes.name: structure
nodes.desc: Among-population genetic structure & connectivity: Fst, gene flow, distinct conservation units / ESUs, identifies which populations are genetically irreplaceable.

nodes.name: genet ramet structure
nodes.desc: Clonal genetic structure: number of distinct genets vs ramets, genet size/age — a clump of '500 plants' may be one ancient genet, drastically changing effective population size.

nodes.name: mycorrhizal association
nodes.desc: Mycorrhizal symbiosis: type (AM/arbuscular — dominant in geophytes; occasionally none/facultative) and, where known, fungal partner identity; obligate vs facultative dependence.

nodes.name: microbiome
nodes.desc: Storage-organ & rhizosphere microbiome: endophytic and rhizosphere bacteria/fungi associated with the bulb/corm — emerging frontier with cultivation and even metabolite-production relevance.

nodes.name: associated community
nodes.desc: Associated plant species / indicator companions co-occurring with the geophyte (the floristic context that signals suitable habitat).

nodes.name: herbivores seed predators
nodes.desc: Herbivores and seed/storage-organ predators: grazing mammals, bulb-eating rodents/wild boar (major geophyte pressure), seed predators, florivores.

nodes.name: pathogens parasites
nodes.desc: Pathogens and parasites: bulb-rot fungi (Fusarium, Botrytis, Sclerotium), viruses, nematodes, parasitic plants — affecting both wild persistence and ex-situ/seed-bank holdings.

nodes.name: mutualists ants
nodes.desc: Non-pollinator mutualists, chiefly ant–seed (myrmecochory) and ant-guarding relationships for elaiosome-bearing geophyte seeds.

nodes.name: wild harvest pressure
nodes.desc: Wild collection / poaching / over-harvest pressure: bulb digging for ornamental trade (Galanthus, Cyclamen, Sternbergia, Eranthis), medicinal harvest (Colchicum, Urginea/Drimia), tonnages, trade routes, CITES listing.

nodes.name: habitat loss drivers
nodes.desc: In-situ habitat-loss drivers at occurrence sites: agriculture/grazing conversion, urbanisation, afforestation of open habitat, mining/quarrying (often on the same limestone/serpentine geophytes need), tourism.

nodes.name: grazing trampling
nodes.desc: Grazing and trampling regime: under-grazing (scrub encroachment shades out open-habitat geophytes) vs over-grazing/trampling damage — geophytes often need an intermediate disturbance regime.

nodes.name: invasive pressure
nodes.desc: Pressure from invasive species at occurrence sites: invasive grasses/shrubs that outcompete native geophytes, or invasive geophytes themselves displacing others.

nodes.name: hydrological alteration
nodes.desc: Hydrological alteration threat: drainage of vernal pools/wet meadows, damming, groundwater abstraction, altered snowmelt — directly disrupts the water regime the geophyte's dormancy clock depends on.

nodes.name: iplc traditional ecological knowledge
nodes.desc: Indigenous & Local Community / traditional ecological knowledge of the geophyte's distribution, seasonality, harvest, and management (e.g. traditional Drimia/Colchicum use, San geophyte-foraging knowledge, Anatolian local harvest calendars)

nodes.name: category
nodes.desc: The species' IUCN Red List category (CR/EN/VU/NT/LC/DD/NE, plus EX/EW) at global scale — the headline conservation status. GEOCON stores this on species.iucn_status AND as the proposed_category of a full iucn_assessments record.

nodes.name: criteria
nodes.desc: The specific Red List criteria codes triggering the category (A1-A4 reduction, B1/B2 restricted-range, C small declining population, D very small population, E quantitative extinction-risk model). GEOCON's IucnAssessmentEditor stores these

nodes.name: aoo eoo
nodes.desc: Spatial extinction-risk parameters: Extent of Occurrence (EOO, minimum convex polygon) and Area of Occupancy (AOO, 2x2 km grid occupancy), number of locations, and severe fragmentation flag — the quantitative inputs to Criterion B.

nodes.name: population
nodes.desc: Population size estimate, structure (mature individuals — for clonal geophytes this means genets not ramets), subpopulation count, and continuing-decline flag. Geophyte-specific subtlety: a 'population' of one bulb that has split into 500 d

nodes.name: generation length
nodes.desc: Generation length (mean age of reproducing individuals) — the multiplier converting an observed decline into the 3-generation window for Criterion A. Geophytes are slow: many bulbs take 4-7+ years from seed to first flowering, so generation

nodes.name: assessment workflow
nodes.desc: The assessment as a living object with a state machine (draft → peer_review → submitted → published) and authorship/peer-review discussion — GEOCON's IUCN Hub. This is the Move that FILLS the category link rather than a data field itself.

nodes.name: history
nodes.desc: The trajectory of Red List status across editions (e.g. VU 2011 → EN 2017 → CR 2023) — genuine vs non-genuine change. Lets the atlas show worsening/improving status over time.

nodes.name: habitat loss
nodes.desc: Habitat conversion/degradation driving decline: agriculture, urbanisation, drainage of wetlands (for rhizomatous/aquatic geophytes), afforestation/ploughing of steppe and meadow (the prime habitat of steppic tulips, crocuses, fritillaries),

nodes.name: overcollection
nodes.desc: Wild harvest / illegal collection / poaching pressure — the threat most specific to geophytes. Ornamental bulb trade (wild-dug snowdrops Galanthus, cyclamen, Sternbergia, tulips, Eranthis), medicinal/food harvest (Crocus for saffron relativ

nodes.name: climate
nodes.desc: Climate-change pressure: warming shifting suitable range upslope/poleward (alpine geophytes have nowhere to go — 'escalator to extinction'), drought stressing Mediterranean geophyte dormancy/flowering, and chilling-requirement failure (many

nodes.name: phenological mismatch
nodes.desc: Decoupling of flowering time from pollinator emergence (or of seed-set from disperser activity) as climate shifts each independently — a pollination-failure threat. Spring geophytes (early-flowering bulbs) are textbook cases because they fl

nodes.name: invasive competition
nodes.desc: Invasive alien plants/animals suppressing native geophytes: invasive grasses/shrubs shading out light-demanding spring bulbs, invasive bulbs themselves (e.g. naturalised ornamental Oxalis, Allium, Muscari) outcompeting natives, and invasive

nodes.name: hybridisation genetic swamping
nodes.desc: Genetic threat: introgression from cultivated/ornamental congeners or from translocated non-local stock swamping a rare endemic's gene pool. Acute for geophyte genera with huge horticultural trade (Tulipa, Narcissus, Crocus, Galanthus, Lili

nodes.name: disease pest
nodes.desc: Pathogen/pest pressure on wild populations: viruses (e.g. potyviruses in Tulipa/Lilium), Fusarium/Botrytis basal rot, nematodes, narcissus bulb fly — threats that can collapse small populations and that also spread via the bulb trade and ex

nodes.name: fire grazing regime
nodes.desc: Disturbance-regime dependence and disruption: many geophytes are fire-followers or grazing-adapted (their underground organ survives what kills competitors), so BOTH fire/grazing suppression AND intensification threaten them. Mediterranean

nodes.name: pollinator disperser loss
nodes.desc: Decline of obligate pollinators or seed dispersers as an indirect threat: specialised geophyte pollination (e.g. buzz-pollinated, deceptive orchid pollination, ant-dispersed/myrmecochorous seeds of many bulbs) means losing the partner cause

nodes.name: locality points
nodes.desc: The georeferenced occurrence/population point set itself — the precise localities of wild populations. This is a DISTINCT, deliberately separated link because it is the single most poaching-sensitive datum in the entire chain and must be ac

nodes.name: protected area overlap
nodes.desc: Whether and how much of the species' range/known sites fall inside formally protected areas (national parks, nature reserves, Natura 2000 / Emerald sites, Key Biodiversity Areas, Important Plant Areas, private reserves) — the in-situ covera

nodes.name: site management
nodes.desc: Active in-place management actions and their status: micro-reserves/plant micro-reserves (a Mediterranean innovation explicitly for narrow endemics incl. geophytes), prescribed burning/grazing, invasive control, fencing against collectors/g

nodes.name: monitoring
nodes.desc: Recurring population monitoring at known sites: census counts, demographic plots, flowering-stem counts over years — the data stream that turns a point population estimate into a trend.

nodes.name: seed banking
nodes.desc: Seed accessions held in seed banks (Millennium Seed Bank, national gene banks, botanic-garden banks) with collection provenance, accession number, seed count and storage location. GEOCON already aggregates this as the 'Seed bank' rollup (se

nodes.name: storage behaviour
nodes.desc: Seed storage physiology: orthodox (desiccation/freeze tolerant, conventionally bankable) vs recalcitrant/intermediate (cannot be dried/frozen — needs cryo or in-vitro). Plus seed dormancy class (many geophytes have deep physiological/morpho

nodes.name: living collection
nodes.desc: Living/field gene-bank holdings: bulbs/corms/tubers grown ex-situ in botanic gardens and field collections, with accession number, provenance and number of individuals. GEOCON aggregates this as the live 'Living collections' rollup (accessi

nodes.name: cryo invitro
nodes.desc: Advanced ex-situ techniques for hard-to-bank geophytes: in-vitro tissue culture / micropropagation, somatic embryogenesis, encapsulation (synthetic seed), and cryopreservation of bulb scales/meristems/embryos in liquid nitrogen. GEOCON alre

nodes.name: genetic representativeness
nodes.desc: How well the ex-situ holding captures the wild genetic diversity: number of source populations/genets sampled, sampling strategy, and gaps. A botanic garden holding 3 ramets of one clone is genetically near-worthless for recovery even thoug

nodes.name: duplication safety
nodes.desc: Whether ex-situ holdings are safety-duplicated across institutions/countries (e.g. a duplicate at Svalbard-equivalent or a partner bank) — insurance against a single collection's loss. Includes the 1S0_compliant 'black-box' duplicate concep

nodes.name: reintroduction
nodes.desc: Establishing a new population within the indigenous range where it had been extirpated, using ex-situ stock. For geophytes this means planting bulbs/corms/tubers or sowing/plug-planting seedlings, with planting protocol and establishment ou

nodes.name: reinforcement
nodes.desc: Augmenting an existing dwindling population with additional individuals (genetic/demographic rescue), as distinct from founding a new one. Requires local-provenance stock to avoid outbreeding depression / swamping.

nodes.name: assisted migration
nodes.desc: Conservation translocation OUTSIDE the historical range to track shifting climate — moving a species (or sourcing climate-adapted provenance) to where its envelope is heading. Highly debated, geophyte-relevant for alpine bulbs with no upslo

nodes.name: outcome monitoring
nodes.desc: Post-intervention monitoring: survival, flowering, seed-set, recruitment of next generation, and self-sustainability of reintroduced/reinforced populations — the evidence that a recovery action actually worked rather than just happened.

nodes.name: national protection
nodes.desc: National/sub-national legal protection status: listed protected species, collection bans, habitat-protection statutes (e.g. national flora-protection laws, schedule listings). Determines whether wild collection is illegal.

nodes.name: listing
nodes.desc: CITES appendix listing (I/II/III) governing international trade. GEOCON already stores species.cites_appendix. Critical geophyte cases: all Orchidaceae are CITES-listed; Galanthus, Sternbergia, Cyclamen, Pachypodium, certain Aloe and Cycads

nodes.name: appendix history
nodes.desc: How a species' CITES listing has changed (up/downlisting, annotations, quotas) and current trade quotas/annotations — the temporal/quantitative governance detail beyond the bare appendix number.

nodes.name: regional directives
nodes.desc: Supra-national conservation listings: EU Habitats Directive Annexes II/IV/V, Bern Convention Appendix I, Emerald Network — which confer legal protection and site-designation obligations across multiple countries. Many Mediterranean/European

nodes.name: permits quotas
nodes.desc: Legal wild-harvest regime where collection is permitted: permit requirements, annual quotas, harvest-season rules, and bulb-size limits. Embodied in schemes like the Turkish controlled snowdrop/bulb export quota system (a real, geophyte-spe

nodes.name: nagoya
nodes.desc: Access and Benefit-Sharing under the Nagoya Protocol/CBD: whether collecting or USING a species' genetic resources (including for extraction/chemistry — the spine's commercial-facing links) requires Prior Informed Consent and Mutually Agree

nodes.name: traditional knowledge
nodes.desc: Indigenous Peoples & Local Communities' associated traditional knowledge and rights over the species: traditional uses (salep tubers, medicinal/edible geophytes), customary harvest practices, and the consent/benefit-sharing and data-soverei

nodes.name: sustainable use certification
nodes.desc: Third-party sustainable-use / sustainable-wild-harvest certification status (FairWild standard, organic-wild) — proof that any wild offtake is at non-detriment levels with social safeguards. The bridge between 'wild-harvested' and 'responsi

nodes.name: organ morphology
nodes.desc: The storage-organ identity and anatomy of the geophyte (true bulb vs corm vs rhizome vs tuber vs tuberous-root/pseudobulb), plus organ-specific traits (tunicate vs scaly bulb, contractile roots, basal plate, replacement-corm habit, stolonif

nodes.name: viability
nodes.desc: Whether the species sets viable seed and at what rate (seed set %, fill rate, embryo presence, cut-test/TZ viability, X-ray fill). Many CR geophytes are self-incompatible, clonal, or have collapsed pollinator mutualisms, so seed propagation

nodes.name: dormancy class
nodes.desc: The seed dormancy class controlling germination: morphological (MD), morphophysiological (MPD — common in Liliaceae/Ranunculaceae geophytes with underdeveloped embryos), physiological (PD), physical (PY), or non-dormant. Geophytes are dorma

nodes.name: germination protocol
nodes.desc: The reproducible recipe that takes viable seed to a germinated seedling: temperature regime, stratification sequence (warm->cold->warm), GA3/KNO3/smoke-water treatments, light/dark, scarification, sowing depth/substrate, and resulting germi

nodes.name: mycorrhizal symbiosis
nodes.desc: Obligate or facilitative fungal/microbial symbiosis required for germination or seedling establishment — the defining bottleneck for terrestrial orchid geophytes (Orchidaceae geophytes need a compatible mycorrhizal fungus for dust-seed germ

nodes.name: seedling to flowering
nodes.desc: Juvenility period: years from germination to first flowering / harvestable storage organ. Geophytes are notoriously slow (Lilium 3-5 yr, many Fritillaria/Tulipa 5-7 yr from seed, some Crocus 3-4 yr). This number determines whether seed prop

nodes.name: natural offsets
nodes.desc: Natural clonal multiplication rate of the storage organ left undisturbed: daughter bulbs/bulblets (tunicate bulbs), cormels around a replacement corm (Crocus/Gladiolus), rhizome branching (Iris), tuber division points, stolon/dropper format

nodes.name: scaling twin scaling
nodes.desc: Assisted vegetative propagation of scaly bulbs by detaching scales (scaling) or scale-pairs on a basal-plate fragment (twin-scaling / chipping), then incubating to induce bulblets at the scale base. Includes chipping (cutting tunicate bulbs

nodes.name: division cuttings
nodes.desc: Rhizome/tuber division (Iris, Canna, Zantedeschia), basal/leaf cuttings that form tuberlets, root cuttings, and Cyclamen/Begonia-style organ sectioning. Mode-specific to non-bulb geophytes that do not scale. Includes wounding/callus-inducti

nodes.name: explant sterilization
nodes.desc: Selection and surface-sterilization of explant tissue for tissue culture: twin-scale fragments, basal-plate discs, scape/peduncle sections, immature embryos, ovary slices, meristem tips. Includes the sterilant regime (NaOCl/HgCl2 concentrat

nodes.name: initiation multiplication
nodes.desc: Culture initiation and the multiplication phase: basal medium (MS/half-MS), PGR balance (cytokinin:auxin, esp. BAP/TDZ for bulblet/shoot multiplication, NAA), and the resulting multiplication factor per subculture cycle. The in-vitro analog

nodes.name: somatic embryogenesis organogenesis
nodes.desc: Regeneration pathway: direct/indirect organogenesis (shoot or bulblet from callus) vs somatic embryogenesis (bipolar embryos), and the genetic-fidelity risk (somaclonal variation) each carries. For conservation, low-somaclonal direct organo

nodes.name: bulblet rooting microtuberization
nodes.desc: In-vitro formation of a storage organ: microbulb/bulblet induction (often by raising sucrose and cytokinin, lowering temperature), microtuberization (potato-model adapted to tuberous geophytes), and rooting of microshoots — converting a sho

nodes.name: acclimatization
nodes.desc: Ex-vitro hardening: weaning microbulbs/plantlets from sterile high-humidity culture to soil, surviving the autotrophic transition and dormancy onset. The notorious survival-cliff where high in-vitro multiplication factors collapse to low ne

nodes.name: slow growth cryo
nodes.desc: In-vitro conservation: minimal/slow-growth storage (reduced temperature, osmotica, growth retardants) and cryopreservation (encapsulation-dehydration, vitrification, droplet-vitrification of shoot tips/bulblets) for long-term ex-situ safegu

nodes.name: dormancy breaking
nodes.desc: Breaking dormancy of the STORAGE ORGAN (distinct from seed dormancy): the warm/cold/photoperiod cues that release bud break and shoot emergence in dormant bulbs/corms/tubers — e.g. tulip/hyacinth cold requirement, programmed cold-weeks for

nodes.name: organ storage
nodes.desc: Post-lift storage conditions for dormant storage organs and propagules: temperature, RH, ventilation, ethylene management, fungicide dusting, and acceptable storage duration before viability loss or rot. Determines whether propagules can be

nodes.name: seed banking
nodes.desc: Seed storage behaviour and ex-situ seed conservation: orthodox vs recalcitrant vs intermediate classification, optimal moisture-content/temperature, predicted longevity, and actual genebank holdings. The conservation backbone of the seed br

nodes.name: substrate
nodes.desc: Growing medium for ex-situ/field cultivation: substrate composition (grit:loam:organic ratios, calcareous vs acidic — many Mediterranean geophytes need sharp-drained alkaline mixes), pH, drainage, pot vs raised bed vs open ground, and depth

nodes.name: water irrigation
nodes.desc: Irrigation regime keyed to the geophyte annual cycle: the critical wet-growth / dry-dormancy alternation that most Mediterranean and arid-zone geophytes obligately require (summer-dry dormancy), watering volume/frequency, and drainage to av

nodes.name: nutrition fertilization
nodes.desc: Fertilization regime: macro/micro nutrient demand timed to the storage-organ filling phase (geophytes load reserves at end of growth), low-N-high-K bulking practice, and mycorrhizal/biofertilizer dependence for nutrient uptake in lean nativ

nodes.name: light climate
nodes.desc: Light, photoperiod, temperature regime, and microclimate (frost tolerance, heat ceiling, alpine vs lowland, shade vs full-sun, snow-cover dependence). Defines whether a species can be grown ex-situ at a given site at all, and the protected-

nodes.name: pests pathogens
nodes.desc: Pest and disease profile under cultivation: fungal/bacterial rots (Fusarium basal rot, Botrytis, Sclerotium, Penicillium blue mould of bulbs), nematodes (stem/bulb nematode Ditylenchus), mites, and storage rots — plus integrated management.

nodes.name: virus indexing
nodes.desc: Virus status and clean-stock production: geophytes accumulate systemic viruses through clonal propagation (tulip breaking virus / potyviruses, LSV/LMoV in Lilium, ornithogalum/iris viruses), so virus indexing (ELISA/RT-PCR) and meristem-tip

nodes.name: phenology cultivated
nodes.desc: The observed annual cycle UNDER CULTIVATION (vs wild phenology): emergence, leafing, flowering, fruiting, senescence, dormancy timing — the calendar that drives every husbandry decision and that GEOCON already models per-month.

nodes.name: vernalization flowering
nodes.desc: Flowering induction and control: vernalization/cold requirement to flower, photoperiod sensitivity, GA-induced flowering, juvenility-to-maturity transition of the storage organ (minimum organ size/weight to flower), and devernalization risk

nodes.name: bulking multiplication rate
nodes.desc: The realised increase: organ size/weight gain per season (bulking) and net multiplication coefficient (organs out per organ in per year) integrating natural offsets + assisted vegetative + husbandry. The single number that tells you how fas

nodes.name: wild to cultivated
nodes.desc: The domestication transition: documented shift of a species from wild-collected to cultivation-sustained supply, including which traits change under cultivation, founder-population genetic capture, and ex-situ-to-production readiness for a

nodes.name: selection crossing
nodes.desc: Breeding activity: controlled crosses, parent selection, interspecific/intergeneric hybridization (common in ornamental geophytes — Lilium, Tulipa, Narcissus, Gladiolus hybrid groups), and selection for traits (flower, organ yield, disease

nodes.name: ploidy mutation marker
nodes.desc: Ploidy manipulation and modern breeding tools applied to geophytes: chromosome counts/flow-cytometry, colchicine polyploidization (widely used to make sterile triploids / vigorous tetraploids in bulbs), mutation breeding (irradiation of bul

nodes.name: cultivar registration
nodes.desc: Formal cultivar development outputs: named cultivars, registration with the relevant ICRA (e.g. KAVB for bulbous ornamentals), DUS (distinctness-uniformity-stability) testing, and plant variety rights / patents on propagation methods.

nodes.name: density rotation cycle
nodes.desc: Field agronomy at production scale: planting density and depth, bed/field system, crop rotation and replant-disease avoidance, mechanization, multi-year cropping cycle length, and labour requirements for field-grown geophytes.

nodes.name: organ grading yield
nodes.desc: Yield and product quality: harvested organ count/weight per area, size grading (circumference grades for bulbs/corms drive market value and flowering capacity), and yield of the target product (flowers, organs, or downstream-extracted-compo

nodes.name: lifting curing grading
nodes.desc: Post-harvest handling: lifting/digging timing relative to senescence, cleaning, curing/drying (to set skins and reduce rot), grading, and counting. The operations that move a field organ into storable/saleable/plantable stock.

nodes.name: forcing programming
nodes.desc: Controlled forcing/programming: pre-cooling and temperature-programming schedules to deliver flowering organs out of natural season (the core of the cut-flower and potted-bulb industry), and dormancy-controlled scheduling of plantable stock

nodes.name: shipping quarantine abs
nodes.desc: Movement and compliance: phytosanitary/quarantine certification for bulb shipment, virus/nematode-free certification, CITES status for protected geophytes (many Galanthus, Cyclamen, Sternbergia are CITES Appendix II), and Nagoya/ABS access-

nodes.name: organ provenance
nodes.desc: Which specific geophyte storage/aerial organ a chemical sample came from, at the resolution geophyte chemistry actually demands: bulb (tunic vs fleshy scale vs basal plate), corm, tuber (cortex vs core), rhizome, contractile/fibrous root, p

nodes.name: harvest state
nodes.desc: Physiological/seasonal state of the organ at sampling — dormant vs sprouting vs flowering vs senescent, and post-harvest handling (fresh, shade-dried, freeze-dried, oven temp). Geophyte storage organs remobilize starch and shift secondary-m

nodes.name: method
nodes.desc: The extraction protocol that liberated compounds from the organ: solvent system (MeOH/EtOH/water/DCM/hexane gradient, acid-base partition for alkaloids), technique (maceration, Soxhlet, ultrasound-UAE, microwave-MAE, supercritical-CO2, acce

nodes.name: yield partition
nodes.desc: Quantitative extract yield and mass balance per organ/solvent — crude extract %, defatted fraction, partition recovery. Lets the chain reason about whether a CR geophyte can ever supply a compound at scale WITHOUT harvesting wild bulbs (de-

nodes.name: fractionation
nodes.desc: Bioassay-guided fractionation / chromatographic separation of the crude extract into fractions and ultimately pure compounds (column chromatography, flash, prep-HPLC, CPC). The step that turns 'this bulb extract is cytotoxic' into 'compound

nodes.name: elucidation
nodes.desc: Determination of the actual molecular structure of an isolated compound — 1D/2D NMR (COSY/HSQC/HMBC/NOESY), HR-MS, IR, UV, optical rotation/ECD for absolute configuration, single-crystal X-ray. For geophytes this is where new Amaryllidaceae

nodes.name: registration
nodes.desc: Stable cross-database identity for the compound: SMILES/InChI/InChIKey, CAS number, PubChem CID, ChEBI, ChEMBL, KNApSAck, COCONUT (natural-products DB) IDs. Turns a local metabolites row into a globally dereplicatable entity.

nodes.name: dereplication
nodes.desc: Distinguishing genuinely NEW geophyte compounds from re-reports of known ones (lycorine, galanthamine, colchicine, tulipalin, proscillaridin all reported thousands of times). A known-vs-novel flag per metabolite record, computed against ext

nodes.name: qual
nodes.desc: Qualitative phytochemical screening / fingerprint of an extract: presence/absence of compound classes (alkaloid, saponin, flavonoid, tannin, cardiac glycoside spot tests) and chromatographic fingerprint (TLC, HPLC-DAD, GC-MS profile) charac

nodes.name: quant
nodes.desc: Targeted quantitative analysis — actual concentration of specific marker compounds (mg/g dry weight): galanthamine in Galanthus/Leucojum/Narcissus, colchicine in Colchicum/Gloriosa, lycorine across Amaryllidaceae, total saponins in Ruscus/A

nodes.name: metabolomics untargeted
nodes.desc: Untargeted LC-MS/MS or NMR metabolomics — the full molecular-feature matrix of an organ/extract, not just named compounds. Enables comparison of whole chemical phenotypes across organs, populations, and species, and discovery of unknowns. G

nodes.name: metabolomics annotation
nodes.desc: Annotation/identification layer over the untargeted features — putative compound IDs from MS/MS spectral matching, in-silico fragmentation (SIRIUS/CSI:FingerID), molecular-network propagation. Bridges raw features to named, dereplicated met

nodes.name: crude screen
nodes.desc: Bioactivity of the CRUDE extract before isolation — broad-panel screening (antioxidant DPPH/ABTS/FRAP, antimicrobial MIC, cytotoxicity on cell lines, enzyme inhibition AChE/BChE/tyrosinase/alpha-glucosidase). The first signal that a geophyt

nodes.name: pure compound
nodes.desc: Quantitative pharmacological activity of an ISOLATED, structurally-defined compound — dose-response, IC50/EC50/Ki against a defined endpoint, with the compound identity nailed down. The difference between 'extract is active' and 'this molec

nodes.name: mechanism
nodes.desc: Molecular target & mechanism of action — what protein/pathway the compound hits and how (galanthamine = reversible AChE inhibitor + nicotinic allosteric modulator; colchicine = tubulin/microtubule poison; proscillaridin = Na+/K+-ATPase card

nodes.name: therapeutic mapping
nodes.desc: Mapping a compound/mechanism to a therapeutic or applied area and its translational stage — disease/condition targeted, cosmetic relevance, agro/biopesticide use, and how far it has progressed (in-vitro / in-vivo / preclinical / clinical tr

nodes.name: profile
nodes.desc: Toxicology & safety of the compound/extract — acute/chronic toxicity, cytotoxicity to normal cells, genotoxicity, the therapeutic window. Critical for geophytes because so many are POISONOUS storage organs: colchicine (lethal, narrow window

nodes.name: ethnobotanical safety
nodes.desc: Traditional/ethnobotanical use and associated safety/preparation knowledge of geophyte organs — e.g. detoxification of acrid Arum/Amorphophallus tubers before eating, traditional medicinal use of Drimia/Urginea (sea squill), Crocus sativus

nodes.name: reference standard
nodes.desc: Existence/availability of an authenticated reference standard for a marker compound (certified standard, in-house purified standard with CoA) — the prerequisite for any honest quantitation. Without a standard, 'quantitative' content claims

nodes.name: marker qc
nodes.desc: Standardization & quality-control profile of a species' extract as a defined material — chosen marker compound(s), acceptance ranges, chromatographic fingerprint for batch identity, stability. The link that makes a geophyte extract a reprod

nodes.name: authentication
nodes.desc: Chemical (and DNA-barcode-cross-checked) authentication / adulteration detection — confirming a sample is the claimed species and not a cheaper/toxic substitute. Acute for geophyte trade: saffron (Crocus sativus) is the world's most adulter

nodes.name: chemotype
nodes.desc: Discrete chemotypes / chemical races within a species — qualitatively different metabolite profiles among populations (presence of one alkaloid skeleton vs another). Well documented in geophytes: Narcissus and Galanthus populations differ i

nodes.name: geographic
nodes.desc: Geographic/edaphic/altitudinal variation in metabolite content across a species' range — quantitative content gradients driven by environment, not genetics. E.g. galanthamine yield varies with altitude/soil; saffron crocin/safranal varies b

nodes.name: seasonal ontogenetic
nodes.desc: Within-individual variation across the dormancy/growth cycle and plant age — how marker content rises and falls through dormant->sprouting->flowering->senescent, and juvenile vs mature bulbs. Defines the optimal sustainable harvest window (

nodes.name: novelty flag
nodes.desc: A first-class flag/record that a metabolite from a given species is NEWLY described (new natural product, or first report from that taxon) — the discovery-credit leaf. Geophytes are still a rich source of novel Amaryllidaceae alkaloids and

nodes.name: pathway
nodes.desc: The biosynthetic pathway producing a geophyte's signature metabolite — precursors, intermediates, key enzymes/genes, and pathway map. Galanthamine and the Amaryllidaceae alkaloids from norbelladine; colchicine from phenylalanine/tyrosine vi

nodes.name: alt supply feasibility
nodes.desc: Feasibility of producing the target compound WITHOUT wild harvest — in-vitro culture/bioreactor (hairy-root, cell-suspension), micropropagation for cultivation supply, semisynthesis, or heterologous expression of pathway genes (yeast/tobacc

nodes.name: environmental regulation
nodes.desc: How environmental/elicitor factors regulate metabolite biosynthesis — stress, jasmonate/elicitor induction, light, temperature, drought, symbionts (mycorrhiza) shifting content. Explains the geographic/seasonal variability mechanistically a

nodes.name: genome size cvalue
nodes.desc: Nuclear genome size (1C/2C DNA content, Mbp/pg) measured per species/accession. Geophytes hold some of the largest plant genomes known (Liliaceae, Amaryllidaceae, Iridaceae) — e.g. Fritillaria, Tulipa, Lilium reach 30-90+ Gbp; this single n

nodes.name: ploidy cytotype
nodes.desc: Ploidy level and cytotype (diploid/triploid/tetraploid/aneuploid, mixoploidy) of a species or population. Geophytes are polyploidy hotspots; many horticulturally important bulbs (Tulipa, Narcissus, Crocus sativus is a sterile triploid) and

nodes.name: chromosome karyotype
nodes.desc: Chromosome number (2n), karyotype morphology, and cytogenetic landmarks (FISH/GISH of rDNA, centromeres, B-chromosomes). Geophyte families have rich classical cytogenetics (e.g. base numbers x=7 in Allium, x=8 in Crocus, dysploidy series);

nodes.name: reference assembly
nodes.desc: A whole-genome assembly (draft → chromosome-scale) for a geophyte species, with N50, BUSCO completeness, annotation. Very few geophytes have reference genomes (Asparagus, garlic Allium sativum, saffron Crocus sativus, some Lilium/Tulipa dra

nodes.name: organellar genomes
nodes.desc: Complete plastome (chloroplast) and mitogenome assemblies. Plastomes are the workhorse for geophyte phylogenomics and super-barcoding; organellar genome structure (IR expansion, gene loss) carries phylogenetic signal across bulb families.

nodes.name: repeat te landscape
nodes.desc: Repetitive-element and transposon characterization — the driver of geophyte genome gigantism. RepeatExplorer/satellite-DNA profiling explains why Fritillaria/Lilium genomes are huge (LTR retrotransposon proliferation, low purging) and provi

nodes.name: de novo
nodes.desc: De novo / reference-guided transcriptome assembly (organ or whole-plant) for species lacking a genome — the practical entry point to gene discovery for most geophytes. Captures expressed genes including biosynthetic enzymes without needing

nodes.name: storage organ atlas
nodes.desc: Tissue/organ-resolved expression atlas of the storage organ itself — bulb scales, corm, tuber, rhizome vs leaf/root/flower. Geophyte-defining: the storage organ is where starch/fructan metabolism, dormancy, and often specialized chemistry (

nodes.name: dormancy sprouting
nodes.desc: Expression dynamics across the dormancy→sprouting→flowering cycle (vernalization, ABA/GA balance, florigen/FT-TFL1, bud-break genes). The molecular control of the geophyte annual cycle — directly actionable for forcing, storage, and cultiva

nodes.name: elicitor stress induced
nodes.desc: Stress/elicitor-responsive transcriptome (jasmonate, wounding, drought, pathogen, MeJA in cell culture) that up-regulates specialized-metabolite pathways. The lever for boosting alkaloid/saponin yield in vitro and for understanding ecologic

nodes.name: singlecell spatial
nodes.desc: Single-cell / spatial transcriptomics of the bulb basal plate, meristem, and storage tissue — resolving where in the organ specialized metabolism and developmental decisions occur. Frontier link; almost no geophyte data yet.

nodes.name: expression proteome
nodes.desc: Protein-level expression (LC-MS/MS proteome, 2D-PAGE) of geophyte organs, complementing transcriptomes where post-transcriptional control matters (storage proteins, dormancy proteins, allergens).

nodes.name: candidate enzymes
nodes.desc: Specific candidate/validated biosynthetic enzymes (sequence + EC number + reaction) for geophyte specialized metabolites — e.g. norbelladine synthase, OMTs and CYPs of Amaryllidaceae-alkaloid biosynthesis, glucosyltransferases of saponins.

nodes.name: pathway genes
nodes.desc: The gene→enzyme→pathway elucidation for a geophyte specialized-metabolite class: the ordered set of genes/enzymes converting precursor to product (e.g. tyrosine→norbelladine→galanthamine in Narcissus/Galanthus; tropane in some; colchicine i

nodes.name: gene cluster
nodes.desc: Physically clustered biosynthetic genes (plant BGC-like operonic arrangements) where present — relevant for some triterpene/saponin and alkaloid pathways. Requires a genome to detect; flags co-regulated, co-located biosynthetic machinery.

nodes.name: barcode identity
nodes.desc: DNA barcode(s) for unambiguous species identification — ITS/ITS2, rbcL, matK, trnH-psbA, and increasingly full-plastome super-barcodes. THE molecular identity token: confirms the taxonomy link and authenticates that an accession/specimen/ex

nodes.name: ssr microsatellite
nodes.desc: SSR/microsatellite marker panels developed or transferred for a geophyte species/genus — the classic codominant markers for parentage, clonality, and population genetics in bulbs (Tulipa, Crocus, Allium, Lilium, Narcissus all have published

nodes.name: snp panel
nodes.desc: Genome-/transcriptome-derived SNP markers and genotyping assays (GBS, RAD-seq, DArTseq, SNP arrays) — the modern high-resolution marker layer for population genomics, GWAS, and genomic selection in geophytes.

nodes.name: cp haplotype
nodes.desc: Chloroplast (and sometimes mitochondrial) haplotype markers and haplotype networks — maternally inherited, ideal for tracing seed-mediated gene flow, glacial refugia, and phylogeographic structure in geophytes.

nodes.name: genetic diversity
nodes.desc: Within-population genetic diversity metrics — expected/observed heterozygosity (He/Ho), allelic richness, polymorphic-loci %, nucleotide diversity. For tiny CR geophyte endemics this is the core extinction-risk signal beyond demography.

nodes.name: population structure
nodes.desc: Among-population genetic structure and gene flow — Fst/Gst, AMOVA, STRUCTURE/ADMIXTURE clusters, isolation-by-distance. Defines how many genetically distinct units a geophyte species comprises and how connected they are.

nodes.name: effective population size
nodes.desc: Effective population size (Ne), recent bottleneck signatures, and Ne/N ratio — the genetic metric of demographic health and adaptive potential. Geophytes' clonality and overlapping generations make Ne estimation distinctive and important.

nodes.name: inbreeding relatedness
nodes.desc: Inbreeding coefficients (Fis), relatedness/kinship, self-incompatibility status, and inbreeding-depression evidence. Critical for geophyte breeding programs and for managing genetic rescue / crossing designs in ex-situ collections.

nodes.name: phylogeography
nodes.desc: Phylogeographic history — refugia, postglacial recolonization routes, lineage divergence dating, comparative phylogeography across co-distributed geophytes. The deep-time context for why diversity is distributed as it is (rich for Mediterra

nodes.name: hybridization introgression
nodes.desc: Detection of natural hybridization, introgression, and reticulate evolution among geophyte species — common in Tulipa, Crocus, Narcissus, Iris where sympatric congeners hybridize, complicating taxonomy and conservation unit definition.

nodes.name: conservation units
nodes.desc: Delimited Evolutionarily Significant Units (ESUs) / Management Units (MUs) / genetic conservation units derived from the population-genetic layer — the actionable synthesis that tells conservation WHICH genetic entities to protect and how t

nodes.name: accession genotyping
nodes.desc: Genotype/fingerprint attached to a specific gene-bank or living-collection accession (the accessions_orgs / seed_lots_orgs already in GEOCON's get_species_domain_extras) — barcode + ploidy + SSR/SNP profile that pins the molecular identity

nodes.name: fingerprinting duplicates
nodes.desc: Molecular fingerprinting across accessions to detect genetic duplicates, mislabels, and off-types in collections — a major real problem in clonally propagated geophyte germplasm (garlic, saffron, ornamental bulbs) where the same clone hides

nodes.name: core collection
nodes.desc: Genetically informed core-collection / minimal-set design — selecting the subset of accessions that captures maximal genetic diversity for efficient conservation, breeding, and screening of a geophyte taxon.

nodes.name: exsitu representativeness
nodes.desc: Genetic gap analysis: how well ex-situ holdings (seed banks, living collections) capture the wild genetic diversity / conservation units of a geophyte — and which populations are missing. Directly couples the conservation-genetics layer to

nodes.name: genetic integrity storage
nodes.desc: Monitoring genetic integrity through ex-situ storage and regeneration — drift/selection during seed regeneration, somaclonal variation in micropropagation, cryopreservation effects on genetic fidelity. Geophytes are often stored as tissue/c

nodes.name: transformation regeneration
nodes.desc: Establishment of genetic transformation + regeneration systems (Agrobacterium / biolistic) for a geophyte — the enabling bottleneck for all functional genomics and editing. Notoriously hard in monocot bulbs; each working protocol is a major

nodes.name: crispr editing
nodes.desc: CRISPR/Cas (and base/prime) gene editing applied to a geophyte — knockouts to validate pathway genes, or trait edits (sterility for biocontainment, flower color, disease resistance). Validates genotype→phenotype/chemotype causally.

nodes.name: vigs rnai
nodes.desc: Transient functional validation — Virus-Induced Gene Silencing (VIGS), RNAi, or transient overexpression — to test pathway-gene function without stable transformation. The practical route in geophytes where stable transformation is hard.

nodes.name: cell culture genetics
nodes.desc: Genetics of in-vitro production systems — callus, cell suspension, hairy-root (Ri T-DNA) and bulblet cultures engineered or elicited to produce geophyte metabolites without wild harvest. The biotech route that relieves poaching pressure on

nodes.name: heterologous expression
nodes.desc: Reconstituting a geophyte biosynthetic pathway in a heterologous host (yeast, Nicotiana, E. coli) to confirm enzyme function and produce the metabolite — the definitive functional proof and a manufacturing route.

nodes.name: synbio chassis
nodes.desc: Full synthetic-biology chassis engineering — multi-gene refactoring, optimized strains, scaled fermentation of a geophyte-derived compound. The translational tip; downstream of confirmed pathways and heterologous expression.

nodes.name: mas breeding
nodes.desc: Marker-assisted selection / genomic-selection genetics for geophyte improvement — linking markers to breeding traits (flower, disease resistance, bulb yield, dormancy) for ornamental and crop geophytes. The applied-breeding genetics layer.

nodes.name: qtl mapping
nodes.desc: Linkage/QTL mapping of phenotypic and chemotypic traits to genomic regions in geophytes — flowering time, dormancy, bulb traits, disease resistance, metabolite content — in structured mapping populations.

nodes.name: mgwas eqtl
nodes.desc: Association genetics for chemistry — metabolite GWAS (mGWAS) and eQTL/co-expression linking natural allelic/expression variation to chemotype across a geophyte's diversity panel. The statistical bridge from the marker/expression layers to t

nodes.name: chemotype haplotype
nodes.desc: Defined chemotype↔genotype associations — haplotypes/alleles/ploidy/cytotypes that predict a geophyte's chemical profile (e.g. high- vs low-galanthamine Narcissus, colchicine-rich Colchicum lineages, saffron apocarotenoid content by clone).

nodes.name: environment plasticity
nodes.desc: Genotype×environment effects on chemotype/phenotype — how the same geophyte genotype shifts metabolite profile or phenology across altitude, soil, climate, and season. Distinguishes heritable chemotype from plastic response — essential befo

nodes.name: provenance authentication
nodes.desc: Molecular provenance/authentication of harvested or traded geophyte material — using barcode + chemotype-marker + ploidy fingerprints to verify species identity AND geographic/clonal origin of bulbs, extracts, and products. The anti-fraud,

nodes.name: folk taxonomy
nodes.desc: Vernacular / folk-taxonomic names per language and community for the geophyte (e.g. Turkish 'çiğdem' for Crocus, 'ters lale' for Fritillaria imperialis, 'salep' collectively for tuberous Orchidaceae). Includes the folk-classification groupi

nodes.name: traditional use
nodes.desc: A recorded traditional/Indigenous use of the geophyte, tagged by use-kind (food, medicine, ritual, dye, fiber, craft, symbolic, forage, poison/insecticide, cosmetic, fodder), with community attribution and region. Geophyte-specific: bulb/co

nodes.name: preparation method
nodes.desc: How the geophyte is traditionally processed and administered: detoxification of toxic bulbs/tubers (leaching cyanogenic glycosides from cassava, boiling Colchicum, roasting camas in earth ovens), decoction/poultice/maceration, dosage form,

nodes.name: bioprospecting lead
nodes.desc: An ethnodirected hypothesis: 'community X uses tuber Y for ailment Z, therefore screen Y's metabolites for activity W'. The formal bridge from ROOT-A back INTO the spine — a traditional medicinal use becomes a prioritized target for the ext

nodes.name: abs provenance
nodes.desc: Nagoya Protocol Access & Benefit-Sharing state for any use of this species' genetic resource or associated traditional knowledge: Prior Informed Consent (PIC) obtained, Mutually Agreed Terms (MAT) signed, Internationally Recognized Certific

nodes.name: biocultural heritage
nodes.desc: The species' role in cultural identity, ritual calendar, festivals, folklore, heraldry, and landscape practice (e.g. Fritillaria/lale in Ottoman art, snowdrop spring festivals, saffron harvest rituals, sego-lily as Utah state emblem). Conse

nodes.name: pharmacology
nodes.desc: Measured pharmacological activity of the species' extract or isolated metabolite: enzyme inhibition (e.g. colchicine→tubulin; galantamine from Galanthus/Narcissus→acetylcholinesterase for Alzheimer's), receptor binding, antimicrobial/antifu

nodes.name: toxicology
nodes.desc: Safety / toxicity profile: acute & chronic toxicity, the famously narrow therapeutic index of colchicine, cardiac glycosides (Drimia/Urginea 'sea squill' — rodenticide & cardiotonic), cyanogenic glycosides in cassava, calcium-oxalate raphid

nodes.name: nutritional
nodes.desc: Nutritional / food-composition profile of edible geophytes: starch type & digestibility (resistant starch in yam/taro), inulin/fructan content (Jerusalem artichoke, camas, chicory-type), protein, micronutrients, glycaemic index, anti-nutrit

nodes.name: organoleptic
nodes.desc: Sensory / organoleptic properties relevant to food, fragrance and flavour: saffron's crocin/safranal/picrocrocin colour-aroma-taste triad, garlic/onion (Allium) thiosulfinate pungency, vanilla-orchid aroma, bitterness/astringency of corms.

nodes.name: agronomic activity
nodes.desc: Biological activity of geophyte extracts/compounds when applied to OTHER organisms in agriculture: biostimulant (root-promoting, stress-priming), allelopathic, biopesticidal/insecticidal (Drimia/squill saponins, Urginea as rodenticide), ant

nodes.name: dermal cosmetic activity
nodes.desc: Skin/hair bioactivity for cosmeceutical use: antioxidant, anti-tyrosinase (skin-lightening), anti-elastase/collagenase (anti-ageing), moisturising mucilage/polysaccharide (salep, snowdrop bulb extracts), soothing. The 'Mathewii Skin Serum'

nodes.name: pharmaceutical
nodes.desc: Use/development of the geophyte (or its metabolite) as a drug or herbal medicine: approved actives (galantamine/Reminyl, colchicine/Colcrys, vinca-type), herbal-medicine monographs, indication, development stage, clinical-trial linkage.

nodes.name: nutraceutical
nodes.desc: Functional-food / dietary-supplement / prebiotic application: inulin from Jerusalem artichoke/camas as prebiotic fibre, saffron mood-supplement capsules, garlic (Allium) cardiovascular supplements, konjac glucomannan (Amorphophallus) weight

nodes.name: cosmeceutical
nodes.desc: Cosmetic / personal-care application: bulb-extract serums and creams (snowdrop, narcissus, tulip, lily), saffron brightening masks, salep-mucilage hair/skin products. INCI-named ingredients sourced from geophytes.

nodes.name: ornamental
nodes.desc: Ornamental / horticultural application — the LARGEST commercial use of geophytes by volume: garden bulbs and cut flowers (tulip, daffodil, hyacinth, lily, crocus, gladiolus, dahlia, snowdrop). Garden-merit, hardiness, forcing behaviour, blo

nodes.name: food
nodes.desc: Direct food / culinary / staple-crop application: taro, yam, cassava, potato, sweet potato, water-chestnut, water-caltrop, camas, arrowroot, and spice geophytes (saffron, garlic, onion, ginger-family rhizomes, turmeric). Staple vs delicacy

nodes.name: agricultural
nodes.desc: Agricultural-input application: geophyte-derived biostimulants, biopesticides/biorodenticides (squill), green-manure, cover/companion geophytes, mycorrhizal-partner value. Also the geophyte AS a crop-improvement genetic resource (crop wild

nodes.name: industrial biomaterial
nodes.desc: Industrial / material application: starch & mucilage as biopolymers/adhesives/films (salep, konjac glucomannan gels, arrowroot/cassava starch for bioplastics & sizing), natural dyes (saffron, Crocus, Iris 'orris'), gums, surfactant saponins

nodes.name: fragrance flavour
nodes.desc: Fragrance & flavour-house application: orris (Iris germanica/pallida rhizome) as the classic perfumery fixative (irones), saffron and vanilla-orchid as flavourings, tuberose, hyacinth absolutes. The high-value aroma-chemistry vertical.

nodes.name: registered
nodes.desc: A named, registered cultivar or selection of the geophyte: ICRA-registered names (KAVB for bulbous ornamentals, the tulip/narcissus/lily/dahlia registers), trade designations, the specific clone behind a product. Distinct from the wild spec

nodes.name: plant breeders right
nodes.desc: Plant Variety Protection / Plant Breeders' Rights granted on a cultivar: UPOV/PVP grant number, CPVO (EU) or national PVP office, holder, grant/expiry dates. The IP instrument specific to ornamental & crop geophytes.

nodes.name: patent
nodes.desc: Patents and published applications touching the species: extraction processes, formulations, isolated-compound uses, propagation methods, biostimulant/cosmetic/pharma claims. The full patent landscape per geophyte.

nodes.name: trademark
nodes.desc: Trademarks and brand names attached to geophyte products/cultivars (trade designations distinct from cultivar epithets, brand lines for serums/supplements). The brand layer above the science.

nodes.name: geographical indication
nodes.desc: Geographical Indication / appellation / protected-origin status tying a geophyte product to a place: Kashmiri/La Mancha/Krokos Kozanis saffron PDO/PGI, regional garlic/onion GIs, terroir-bound bulb provenance.

nodes.name: dossier
nodes.desc: Regulatory status & registrations enabling lawful sale: novel-food authorisation (EU 2015/2283), herbal-medicine registration (EMA HMPC), cosmetic CPNP notification, dietary-supplement notifications, biopesticide registration. The complianc

nodes.name: on market
nodes.desc: A concrete product/service in commerce derived from the species: the cream, supplement, spice pack, bulb SKU, plant material, productized assay service. The terminal commercial citation — recorded, never transacted.

nodes.name: sourcing mode
nodes.desc: How raw material is obtained: wild-harvested vs field-cultivated vs tissue-culture/micropropagated vs gene-bank. The pivotal conservation↔commerce variable — wild-harvest of CR geophytes (snowdrops, cyclamen, sternbergia, salep orchids, squ

nodes.name: cites trade
nodes.desc: CITES listing & international trade-volume status for the species: Appendix I/II/III, annual export quotas, CITES Trade Database records. Many heavily-traded ornamental/medicinal geophytes are CITES-listed (Galanthus, Cyclamen, Sternbergia,

nodes.name: certification
nodes.desc: Sustainability / ethical-sourcing certification on the product or its supply: FairWild (the standard for wild-collected medicinal/aromatic plants), organic, Fair Trade, Rainforest Alliance, MSC-equivalent botanical schemes.

nodes.name: traceability
nodes.desc: Chain-of-custody linking the marketed product back to a specific accession / seed-lot / cultivated origin — the literal welded 'Connection edge' of THE CHAIN extended into commerce: same accession flows propagation→cultivation→…→product, ea

nodes.name: wild harvest pressure
nodes.desc: Documented collection/poaching pressure and population impact from commercial demand: salep-orchid tuber harvest collapse, snowdrop/cyclamen bulb digging, sternbergia/squill exploitation, saffron-substitute adulteration pressure. The harm s

nodes.name: benefit sharing
nodes.desc: The flow-back of value to the source country / Indigenous-or-local community: monetary & non-monetary benefit-sharing under Nagoya MAT, GI-based community returns, FairWild premiums, capacity-building. The ethical closing of the loop the fi
